saudi_arabia_crude: Saudi crude exports recovered to ~4.5 mb/d (23–28 Mar) from the 2.8 mb/d trough (9–15 Mar) as Aramco's East-West Pipeline hit full 7 mb/d capacity, routing all Yanbu-bound crude to the Red Sea — but still ~30% below the 6.4 mb/d shipped in the first week of the crisis.

Customer mix has dramatically narrowed: China (1.3 mb/d) and India (1.2 mb/d) now absorb ~55% of all Saudi seaborne crude, with Myanmar emerging as a new buyer at 335 kbd — while Japan, South Korea, and Egypt have disappeared from the latest week, likely drawing on SPR releases instead.

The Houthi threat to close Bab al-Mandeb is the key risk: Yanbu is Saudi's only functioning export terminal, and a Red Sea blockade would effectively shut the kingdom's entire 4.5 mb/d export corridor with no remaining bypass option.
saudi_arabia_gasoil_diesel: Saudi diesel exports declined to ~593 kbd (23–28 Mar) from ~743 kbd pre-crisis, a 20% drop reflecting reduced refinery throughput as crude is prioritised for pipeline-to-Yanbu exports over domestic refining.

Buyer geography shifted dramatically westward: Belgium surged to top buyer at 124 kbd — consistent with Red Sea routing via Suez — while traditional Asian buyers (France, Spain, Italy) rotated in and out weekly as European buyers competed for Yanbu-sourced cargoes.
Egypt remains the most consistent buyer across all four weeks (80–191 kbd), likely reflecting geographic proximity to Yanbu and critical domestic fuel needs.
saudi_arabia_gasoline: Saudi gasoline exports halved to ~102 kbd (23–28 Mar) from 224 kbd pre-crisis, with only Kenya and Singapore taking cargoes — a sign that Yanbu pipeline capacity is being prioritised for crude over refined products.

The volatile week-to-week swings (138→275→102 kbd) suggest opportunistic cargo allocation rather than stable supply commitments, consistent with Aramco managing scarce pipeline capacity on a transaction-by-transaction basis.
saudi_arabia_kero_jet: Saudi jet fuel exports collapsed to zero (23–28 Mar) after averaging ~80 kbd across the prior three weeks, with cargoes routed via Yanbu to European buyers (UK, Netherlands) now apparently halted.
Jet fuel is likely being deprioritised in favour of crude and diesel for the limited Yanbu pipeline capacity; any Houthi escalation in the Red Sea would eliminate this export route entirely.
saudi_arabia_naphtha: Saudi naphtha exports held at ~85 kbd (23–28 Mar), a small but notable flow routed entirely to Indonesia — providing a lifeline to Southeast Asian petrochemical crackers starved of Gulf naphtha after the YNCC and Chandra Asri force majeures.

The shift from Japan (pre-crisis buyer) to Indonesia and Singapore reflects Yanbu-to-Red Sea routing: Southeast Asian buyers accessible via Suez are replacing traditional Northeast Asian customers who relied on Gulf-loaded cargoes.
uae_crude: UAE crude exports staged a strong V-shaped recovery to ~3.2 mb/d (23–28 Mar), matching pre-crisis levels, as ADNOC ramped the ADCOP pipeline to Fujairah near its 1.8 mb/d surge capacity — with Fujairah loadings averaging 1.9 mb/d in late March per Kpler tracking.

India vaulted to top buyer at 1.1 mb/d (34% share), displacing Thailand which had dominated earlier weeks at 550–829 kbd — consistent with Indian Navy escort convoys under Operation Urja Suraksha creating a secure corridor from Fujairah.

South Korea (642 kbd) and Japan (317 kbd) reappeared as buyers in 23–28 Mar after disappearing in 16–22 Mar, suggesting Asian SPR-reliant importers are now re-entering the spot market as reserves deplete.

IRGC strikes on Al Taweelah aluminium (Mar 29) and earlier Fujairah infrastructure damage remain the key downside risk — any direct hit on the ADCOP terminal or Fujairah loading berths would remove the UAE's only Hormuz bypass.
uae_gasoil_diesel: UAE diesel exports collapsed to zero for the last two weeks (16–28 Mar) after modest flows of 40–73 kbd in early March, consistent with Ruwais Refinery-2 going offline and the Fujairah bunkering hub FM shutting marine fuel deliveries.

With Bapco (Bahrain) and Kuwait's Al-Ahmadi also offline, the entire Gulf refined products complex east of Hormuz is effectively shut — leaving Saudi Yanbu as the sole Gulf-origin diesel export point.
uae_gasoline: UAE gasoline exports went to zero from 9 Mar onward after 140 kbd in the first crisis week (2–8 Mar), reflecting the combined impact of Ruwais Refinery-2 shutdown and Fujairah port disruption from the MEE and Pearl Marine force majeures on bunker fuel.
Recovery is unlikely until either Hormuz reopens or Ruwais is brought back online — ADNOC is clearly prioritising crude-for-export over refinery runs given ADCOP pipeline capacity constraints.
uae_naphtha: UAE naphtha exports resumed at 54 kbd to Taiwan (23–28 Mar) after two weeks of zero flows, likely a single Fujairah-loaded cargo reaching a naphtha-starved Taiwanese cracker.
Pre-crisis flows were 80 kbd to China; the shift to Taiwan suggests opportunistic spot sales as Asian petrochemical operators scramble for any available non-Hormuz naphtha at steep premiums.
iraq_crude: Iraq's crude exports suffered the most catastrophic collapse in this crisis: from 2.2 mb/d (2–8 Mar) to just 250 kbd (23–28 Mar), a 89% plunge driven by the Mar 20 FM on all foreign-operated oilfields that cratered Basra output to ~900 kbd from 3.3 mb/d.

The only functioning export route is the Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline to Turkey, now running at ~250 kbd — all seaborne exports via Basra/Umm Qasr have ceased, and China, India, Italy, and Malaysia (the pre-crisis buyers) have completely disappeared.

Even if Hormuz reopened tomorrow, Iraq's production recovery would lag by months: BP has been ordered to cut North Rumaila to 350 kbd, and storage at Basra terminals is reportedly full, requiring a prolonged ramp-up sequence.
iraq_naphtha: Iraqi naphtha exports are negligible at ~15 kbd sporadically — Iraq has minimal naphtha export infrastructure, and with Basra refineries running at a fraction of capacity under the FM, there is effectively no exportable surplus.
qatar_crude: Qatar's crude exports have effectively ceased: zero in the latest week (23–28 Mar) and only a single 117 kbd cargo to the UAE (16–22 Mar) since the initial 292 kbd in the first crisis week, consistent with QatarEnergy's comprehensive FM on all hydrocarbon exports.

All offshore oil fields (~440 kbd normal capacity) remain shut with Laffan Refineries 1 and 2 offline; the small UAE-bound cargo was likely a short-haul delivery from existing floating storage rather than new production.
qatar_lng: Qatar LNG exports collapsed from ~53 ktons/d (2–8 Mar) to effectively zero, with only minimal flows to Kuwait (6.5–21.5 ktons/d) in the last two weeks — likely short-haul deliveries from pre-loaded cargoes, not new production from the shuttered 77 Mtpa complex.

The Mar 27 Edison FM notification (10 cargoes through mid-June) confirms this is not a temporary disruption: ~20% of global LNG supply is offline indefinitely, with 12.8 Mtpa of capacity damaged for 3–5 years from the Mar 18 missile strikes on Ras Laffan.

The Kuwait-only destination pattern in recent weeks is notable — Kuwait's Al-Zour FSRU desperately needs gas for domestic power, and Qatar appears to be directing its handful of available cargoes to its closest Gulf neighbour rather than honouring Asian long-term contracts.
qatar_naphtha: Qatar naphtha exports zeroed out after a single 16.5 kbd cargo to Japan in the first crisis week (2–8 Mar) — with all downstream NGL processing at Ras Laffan halted, there is no feedstock to produce exportable naphtha.
iran_crude: Iran's crude exports declined steadily from ~1.85 mb/d (2–8 Mar) to ~1.33 mb/d (23–28 Mar), a 28% drop — despite Iran controlling Hormuz, Israeli strikes on South Pars Phases 3–6, 14, and 17–18 have taken ~12% of gas-linked output offline, constraining upstream production.

Destination data shows only China as a visible buyer (658 kbd in W10), with subsequent weeks showing no country-level breakdown — consistent with Iran's dark fleet operations and ship-to-ship transfers that obscure final destinations, likely still overwhelmingly China-bound.

Iran is charging ~$2M/vessel in yuan for IRGC-tolled passage through Hormuz, creating a de facto revenue stream from the blockade while simultaneously striking UAE and Bahrain infrastructure (Al Taweelah, Alba smelter on Mar 29) to deter competitors' bypass routes.
iran_naphtha: Iran's naphtha exports are minimal and sporadic (13–46 kbd in the first two weeks, then zero), with a single UAE-bound cargo in 9–15 Mar — suggesting opportunistic intra-Gulf sales rather than a structured export programme.
kuwait_crude: Kuwait's crude exports have been virtually eliminated: from 1.16 mb/d (2–8 Mar) to zero in the latest week, after the KPC force majeure and the devastating Iranian drone strikes on Al-Ahmadi refinery (730 kbd capacity) on Mar 19–20.

A tiny 78 kbd was recorded in 16–22 Mar — likely a pre-loaded cargo departing before the Al-Ahmadi strikes — but with storage filling and no Hormuz transit possible, Kuwait has no functioning export route.
KPC has warned of 3–4 months to restore full Al-Ahmadi capacity even post-war, meaning Kuwait's export recovery will be the slowest among Gulf producers.
kuwait_gasoil_diesel: Kuwait's diesel exports collapsed to zero from 16 Mar onward after 102–134 kbd in the first two weeks, with the Al-Ahmadi refinery destruction eliminating all refined product output.
The early-crisis flows were entirely intra-Gulf (UAE and Qatar), suggesting emergency regional supply-sharing before the refinery strikes made even that impossible.
kuwait_gasoline: Kuwait's gasoline exports were negligible throughout: a single 64 kbd cargo to Saudi Arabia (9–15 Mar) was the only recorded flow, likely an intra-Gulf transfer before the Al-Ahmadi destruction ended all Kuwaiti refining.
oman_crude: Oman is the clear winner among Gulf crude exporters: exports rebounded to ~1.27 mb/d (23–28 Mar), surpassing even the pre-crisis 1.1 mb/d, as Mina Al-Fahal's position outside the Hormuz chokepoint gives Oman a unique strategic advantage.

China takes virtually 100% of Omani crude across all four weeks (occasionally sharing with Taiwan), highlighting Oman's role as a reliable non-Hormuz Gulf barrel for Chinese refiners scrambling to replace lost Saudi, Iraqi, and Kuwaiti supply.

The Mar 29 Salalah Port strike is the key risk — while Mina Al-Fahal (the crude terminal) is on the Gulf of Oman coast, escalation of strikes against Omani infrastructure could threaten the only functioning non-bypass Gulf crude export hub.
oman_lng: Oman LNG maintained relatively stable exports of 25–43 ktons/d throughout March, commanding significant Asian spot premiums as the only functioning Gulf LNG exporter with Qatar's entire 77 Mtpa offline.

Buyer mix rotated across India, Japan, South Korea, Thailand, Taiwan, and China — reflecting fierce competition for Oman's limited 10.4 Mtpa capacity, with India the most consistent buyer (appearing in 3 of 4 weeks).
At current rates (~35 ktons/d avg), Oman is running near nameplate capacity; there is no spare LNG volume to offset even a fraction of Qatar's ~211 ktons/d loss.
oman_gasoil_diesel: Oman's diesel exports held at 50–144 kbd across March, serving a remarkably diverse customer base spanning Pakistan, South Africa, Mauritius, Namibia, Madagascar, and Argentina — benefiting from its non-Hormuz position to supply African and South Asian markets starved of Gulf diesel.

Pakistan emerged as the most consistent buyer (appearing in 3 of 4 weeks at 49–59 kbd), likely reflecting Oman's geographic proximity and Pakistan's critical dependence on Gulf refined product imports.
oman_gasoline: Oman's gasoline exports rose to 140 kbd (23–28 Mar), the highest of the four-week period, with Pakistan (85 kbd) and Saudi Arabia (55 kbd) as buyers — the Saudi purchase is particularly notable, suggesting the kingdom is importing Omani gasoline to meet domestic demand as its own refineries prioritise crude-for-export.

Oman's Sohar and Duqm refinery complex, safely outside the conflict zone, has become the de facto products hub for the western Indian Ocean region, serving East Africa (Tanzania, Madagascar, Sudan) and South Asia.
oman_kero_jet: Oman's jet fuel exports are small and sporadic (8–25 kbd), with cargoes to Mauritius and Djibouti reflecting niche Indian Ocean bunkering demand rather than major trade flows.
oman_naphtha: Oman naphtha exports dropped from 116 kbd (2–8 Mar) serving India and South Korea to zero in the last two weeks, possibly reflecting diversion of NGL feedstock to higher-margin LNG and gasoline production as Oman prioritises its most valuable export streams.
South Korea was the primary buyer in the first two weeks (70–72 kbd), consistent with Korean naphtha crackers scrambling for any non-Hormuz supply after declaring FM on Middle East naphtha (YNCC).
bahrain_kero_jet: Bahrain's jet fuel exports ceased entirely after a single 72 kbd cargo to the UAE in the first crisis week (2–8 Mar) — the Bapco Sitra refinery (405 kbd) was struck by Iranian missiles on Mar 4 and again Mar 9, destroying all refining capacity and triggering Bapco Energies' comprehensive FM.
The IRGC's third strike on Bahrain (Alba aluminium, Mar 29) confirms the country's industrial base is being systematically targeted; refinery reconstruction is a multi-year prospect.
bahrain_naphtha: Bahrain's naphtha exports went to zero after a single 73 kbd cargo to Japan in the first crisis week, directly caused by the destruction of Sitra refinery — Bahrain's entire refining and NGL processing infrastructure is offline indefinitely under the Bapco Energies force majeure.
russia_crude: Russia's crude exports surged 41% to ~6.8 mb/d (9–15 Mar) as panic buying peaked amid Gulf supply losses, then retreated to ~4.9 mb/d (23–28 Mar) — still above the pre-crisis ~4.8 mb/d baseline, with Urals crude nearly doubling to ~$90/bbl per Carnegie/Bloomberg.

China and India absorbed the lion's share: China took 1.3 mb/d and India 454 kbd (23–28 Mar), while European buyers (Italy, France, Netherlands) each drew 180–295 kbd — enabled by a 30-day US sanctions waiver for Russian crude loaded before 12 Mar per Global Trade Review.

The customer base broadened dramatically mid-crisis — Turkey, Philippines, Croatia, and Hong Kong all appeared as new buyers in March, as 56% of Russian crude moved on shadow-fleet tankers outside G7 maritime services per CREA data.

Risk: EU sanctions waiver expires in April and the EU Russian LNG purchase ban takes effect 25 Apr; any tightening of enforcement could redirect Russian crude back toward deep-discount Asian buyers, compressing Moscow's windfall margins.
russia_lng: Russian LNG exports held broadly flat at 81–111 ktons/d through March, with Europe (Spain, France, Belgium, Netherlands) absorbing the bulk — consistent with CREA data showing Yamal LNG sent 100% of February exports to the EU.
Japan and South Korea appeared as buyers in W11 and W13 (19–21 ktons/d), likely taking spot cargoes at premium as Qatar's 77 Mtpa remained fully offline.

The EU ban on Russian LNG purchases takes effect 25 Apr — once enforced, ~80–110 ktons/d of supply will need to find new Asian buyers or be curtailed, tightening the already strained global LNG market further.
russia_gasoil_diesel: Russian diesel exports collapsed 71% from 932 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 236 kbd (23–28 Mar), with the customer base shrinking to just Turkey (111 kbd) and Togo (43 kbd) — down from seven destinations earlier in the month.
Turkey remains Russia's anchor diesel buyer throughout March at 290–410 kbd early in the month, absorbing nearly half of all flows; Egypt surged to 181 kbd in W11 but disappeared thereafter.

The sharp contraction likely reflects a combination of Russian refinery seasonal maintenance and tightening sanctions compliance, though Brazil's exit as a buyer (~140 kbd in W12, zero in W13) warrants monitoring.
russia_gasoline: Russian gasoline exports effectively ceased by mid-March — volumes fell from a negligible 38 kbd (exclusively to Libya) in early March to zero from 16 Mar onward, reflecting both limited export-grade production and Russia's domestic supply retention policies.
russia_naphtha: Russian naphtha exports declined 55% from 632 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 252 kbd (23–28 Mar), with only Turkey remaining as a buyer in the latest week — down from six destinations including China (231 kbd), India (155 kbd), and South Korea earlier in the month.

The mid-March spike to China and India coincided with the shutdown of ME naphtha supply chains (YNCC in South Korea and Chandra Asri in Indonesia both declared FM on naphtha feedstock), making Russian naphtha a critical alternative before volumes faded.

If the customer base remains this narrow, it signals either logistics constraints on Russia's Baltic/Black Sea naphtha routes or Asian buyers pivoting to US Gulf Coast naphtha which surged to 738 kbd in W12.
us_crude: US crude exports rose steadily through March to 4.3 mb/d (23–28 Mar), up 19% from 3.65 mb/d at the start of the month, as the US became the de facto swing supplier for Gulf-displaced barrels — per EIA, SPR lending is running at ~1.4 mb/d from three Gulf Coast sites.

Asian buyers surged: South Korea took 557 kbd (23–28 Mar, up from 116 kbd in W10), Japan 319 kbd, India 290 kbd, and Taiwan 299 kbd in mid-March — collectively replacing ~1.2 mb/d of lost Gulf crude supply.

European flows remained robust with Netherlands (583 kbd), UK (233 kbd), and Spain (234 kbd) as consistent buyers, reflecting the Atlantic Basin's emergence as the primary crude supply corridor with Hormuz and potentially Bab al-Mandeb both constrained.

Goldman warns $135/bbl Brent if both Hormuz and Bab al-Mandeb close simultaneously — US export capacity is near maximum and further upside would require either OPEC spare capacity releases or deeper SPR draws.
us_lng: US LNG exports ran flat at ~367 ktons/d through March — effectively at maximum terminal capacity across Sabine Pass, Freeport, Cameron, Corpus Christi, and Plaquemines — with Cheniere now the world's largest LNG exporter following Qatar's 77 Mtpa shutdown per Fortune/Platts.

Destination mix shifted toward Asia as the crisis deepened: Thailand (40 ktons/d), Bangladesh (12 ktons/d), and India (20 ktons/d) all appeared as US LNG buyers in March, markets previously served primarily by Qatar.

Europe remained the dominant destination (Netherlands, France, Germany, Spain, Italy collectively ~150–170 ktons/d), but the ~20% global LNG supply gap from Qatar cannot be filled at current US capacity — Golden Pass LNG's first cargo is the only near-term uplift, expected imminently per EIA.
us_lpg: US LPG exports rose 23% to 176 ktons/d (23–28 Mar) as Enterprise and Targa Gulf Coast terminals captured demand displaced by the near-total shutdown of Saudi, UAE, and Qatari NGL supply chains per Argus LPG World.

Indonesia surged to top buyer at 13.4 ktons/d (23–28 Mar), overtaking Japan (11.3 ktons/d), as Asian PDH plant operators scrambled for US propane cargoes with ME supply offline — the LPG-naphtha spread now heavily favours US exports.

Mexico remained a steady base-load buyer (~12–17 ktons/d), but the broadening Asia customer base (China, India, South Korea, Taiwan, Vietnam all appearing through March) signals a structural shift in US LPG trade flows that may persist post-crisis.
us_kero_jet: US jet fuel exports surged 126% from 154 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 346 kbd (16–22 Mar) before easing to 219 kbd (23–28 Mar), as transatlantic kero flows partially replaced the ~470 kbd of ME aviation kerosene that previously supplied European and Asian airports per Bruegel.

The UK emerged as the dominant buyer at 102 kbd (23–28 Mar), and the Netherlands at 50 kbd — both previously served by ME refineries now offline; France also took 47 kbd in W11, signalling broad European re-sourcing.

LatAm/Caribbean routes (Panama, Costa Rica, Mexico) remained steady base-load demand, suggesting US refiners are layering new European volumes on top of existing Western Hemisphere contracts rather than diverting.
us_gasoil_diesel: US diesel exports climbed 52% from 909 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 1.38 mb/d (23–28 Mar), with the Netherlands surging to 467 kbd — up from just 11 kbd in W10 — as Europe scrambled to replace lost ME middle distillates per the IEA Oil Market Report.

The UK doubled to 151 kbd (23–28 Mar) and Morocco, South Africa, and Peru all drew consistent volumes, reflecting how US Gulf Coast refinery output is now flowing to destinations across four continents simultaneously.

This 52% ramp in a single month is remarkable given US diesel stocks were already seasonally tight; sustained flows at this level risk tightening domestic US distillate markets and pushing pump prices higher.
us_gasoline: US gasoline exports rose to 923 kbd (23–28 Mar), with Mexico remaining the dominant buyer at 277 kbd (~30% share) — largely unchanged from pre-crisis trade patterns reflecting pipeline and short-haul tanker infrastructure.

Australia appeared as a new buyer at 51–64 kbd in W12–W13, sourcing US gasoline for the first time in the tracked period — likely driven by the disruption to ME refined product flows that previously supplied Southeast Asian and Australasian markets.

LatAm/Caribbean destinations (Colombia, Dominican Republic, Jamaica, Panama) accounted for the balance, suggesting US gasoline exports are demand-constrained by regional refinery deficits rather than the Hormuz crisis.
us_naphtha: US naphtha exports spiked 121% to 738 kbd (16–22 Mar) as South Korea (203 kbd) and Japan (126 kbd) made emergency purchases — South Korea's surge is directly linked to YNCC's FM declaration on ethylene production after total ME naphtha supply suspension.

Volumes eased to 439 kbd (23–28 Mar) with Venezuela (84 kbd), Colombia (53 kbd), and Japan (38 kbd) as buyers, suggesting the initial panic buying wave has subsided but structural demand from Asia remains.

The Asia naphtha scramble underscores the depth of the petrochemical feedstock crisis: Chandra Asri (Indonesia), YNCC (South Korea), and PCS (Singapore) all declared FM on ME-linked feedstock, and US Gulf Coast is now a primary alternative alongside Russian Baltic exports.
eu_27_crude: EU-27 crude exports are negligible and declining — from 586 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 168 kbd (23–28 Mar) — reflecting that Europe is a net crude importer; the small outflows (Finland, Netherlands, Turkey) are likely re-exports or intra-refinery transfers rather than production-based trade.
These volumes are not strategically significant in the context of the ~10 mb/d Gulf supply loss.
eu_27_kero_jet: EU-27 jet fuel exports collapsed 83% from 146 kbd (9–15 Mar) to just 12 kbd (23–28 Mar, UK only), as European refiners retained virtually all kero/jet production for domestic consumption with ME aviation kerosene supply (~470 kbd through Hormuz) fully offline.

The retention signals acute tightness in European jet fuel markets — airlines are likely facing escalating procurement costs, and long-haul routes to Asia face particular pressure given the loss of Gulf hub bunkering at Fujairah (also under FM).
eu_27_gasoil_diesel: EU-27 diesel exports spiked to ~1.08 mb/d (9–22 Mar) before dropping 61% to 424 kbd (23–28 Mar), as internal EU rebalancing slowed and member states likely began retaining more supply ahead of potential prolonged Gulf disruption.

The destination mix remained predominantly intra-European and near-neighbourhood (UK, Poland, Croatia, Slovenia, Morocco), with no significant long-haul flows — consistent with EU refiners prioritising regional energy security over export margins.
Egypt and Lebanon appeared as new diesel destinations in W11–W12, likely reflecting emergency supply for countries heavily dependent on ME refined product imports that have been cut off.
eu_27_gasoline: EU-27 gasoline exports expanded 29% to 1.18 mb/d (23–28 Mar), with a dramatic geographic shift toward Africa — Ghana, Senegal, Mauritania, Morocco, and Kenya all became major buyers, replacing ME-sourced gasoline that previously served these markets.

Saudi Arabia appeared as a gasoline importer from the EU in W11 (75 kbd), a remarkable reversal for the world's largest oil exporter — consistent with reduced Saudi refinery output as Ras Tanura and Jubail complexes prioritise crude-for-export over domestic product runs.

The EU's ability to sustain >1 mb/d gasoline exports depends on refinery run rates staying elevated; if crude supply tightens further (Brent now >$112), European margins could compress and force export cuts.
eu_27_naphtha: EU-27 naphtha exports held at 272–423 kbd through March, with Asian buyers appearing mid-crisis: China (73 kbd in W11, 58 kbd in W13), Japan (50 kbd in W12), and Taiwan (46 kbd in W11) all sourced European naphtha for the first time in the tracked period.

This Asian pull reflects the desperation of petchem feedstock buyers cut off from ME naphtha — YNCC (South Korea), Chandra Asri (Indonesia), and Wanhua Chemical (China) all declared FM on Middle East supply.
Intra-EU flows (Belgium, Netherlands, Spain, Italy) remained the base, but the new Asian corridor could persist if the Hormuz closure extends and ME naphtha remains offline.
australia_crude: Australian crude exports averaged 210–311 kbd through March, a small but steady flow directed entirely at the Asia-Pacific: South Korea was the dominant buyer at 124 kbd (23–28 Mar), with Singapore and Thailand rounding out earlier weeks.

These volumes are modest (~0.2–0.3 mb/d) relative to the Gulf supply gap but represent premium light crudes (condensate/light sweet) that are difficult to substitute, giving Australian producers pricing power in the current environment.
australia_lng: Australian LNG exports trended down from 252 ktons/d (9–15 Mar) to 157 ktons/d (23–28 Mar), driven partly by declining infrastructure capacity and partly by Australia-Singapore energy security agreements to prioritise regional supply per The Diplomat.

Japan remained the anchor buyer at 48–83 ktons/d, but the customer base diversified: India appeared for the first time (9 ktons/d in 23–28 Mar), Thailand rose to 23 ktons/d, and Bangladesh to 11 ktons/d — all markets previously served primarily by Qatar.

With Qatar's 77 Mtpa (~211 ktons/d) offline and Australian LNG declining, the ~20% global supply gap is widening further — Australia's aging Northwest Shelf and limited expansion capacity mean US LNG is the only scalable alternative, but it is already running at full utilisation.
australia_naphtha: Australian naphtha exports are sporadic and negligible — a single cargo to Singapore (49 kbd, W11) and one to Malaysia (58 kbd, W13) — reflecting that Australia has minimal naphtha export infrastructure and is not a meaningful alternative source for Asia's petchem feedstock crisis.
china_crude: China's crude imports fell 41% from ~14.5 mb/d (2–8 Mar) to ~8.5 mb/d (23–28 Mar) as the Hormuz closure progressively strangled Gulf supply — Gulf share collapsed from 44% to 29%, with Saudi Arabia and Kuwait both vanishing entirely from the latest week's cargo manifests.

Russia remained the sole reliable anchor at ~2.3–2.7 mb/d throughout the crisis (per CREA tracking), while Oman surged from 1.1 mb/d to 1.8 mb/d leveraging its non-Hormuz export position — together accounting for nearly half of China's remaining supply.

Iraq's crude to China cratered from 1.7 mb/d to just 85 kbd (23–28 Mar), consistent with the Mar 20 FM on all foreign-operated oilfields that left Basra at 900 kbd (from 3.3M bpd). Diversification to West Africa and Latin America is underway but cannot compensate for the ~6 mb/d shortfall.

Despite China's 'friendly nation' Hormuz transit status, COSCO vessels were turned back per Bloomberg — undercutting assumptions that bilateral deals would insulate Chinese imports. Sinopec's 10% refinery run cuts and China's Mar 11 refined fuel export ban signal domestic rationing has begun.
china_lng: Qatar LNG — which supplied 52 ktons/d to China in early March — disappeared entirely by 23–28 Mar following QatarEnergy's FM extension to Chinese contracts on Mar 24, removing China's single largest LNG supplier overnight.

Australia stepped into the breach as the dominant supplier at 43 ktons/d (54% of total), but overall volumes still fell 26% to ~81 ktons/d as the impossibility of replacing ~20% of global LNG supply in weeks becomes apparent. Spot LNG costs surged ~60% to $25/MMBtu per JKM benchmarks, inflating China's import bill even as volumes contract.

Pipeline gas from Power of Siberia provides partial insulation, but the remaining LNG suppliers (Malaysia 17 ktons/d, Indonesia 10 ktons/d, Russia 11 ktons/d) lack the export capacity to fill Qatar's 52 ktons/d gap — a structural shortfall likely to persist until the conflict ends.
china_lpg: China's LPG imports whipsawed between 46–78 ktons/d through March as the world's largest LPG importer scrambled to replace lost Gulf NGL supply — Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia all dropped out by 23–28 Mar, leaving the US (39 ktons/d) and Iran (18 ktons/d) as the dominant pair.

Iran's sustained LPG deliveries (~18 ktons/d) reflect the bilateral transit arrangement granting Chinese vessels Hormuz passage, though reliability remains uncertain given ongoing military activity. US Gulf Coast exports from Enterprise and Targa terminals are the primary replacement source per Argus LPG World.
china_kero_jet: China's jet fuel imports dried up completely after 15 Mar — the sole supplier (South Korea, at ~49 kbd early March) ceased deliveries as Seoul moved to conserve refined products for domestic use, formalizing a naphtha export ban on Mar 27. Sinopec's 10% refinery run cuts likely compound the domestic jet fuel shortfall.
china_gasoil_diesel: China's diesel imports collapsed 86% from ~99 kbd (2–8 Mar) to ~14 kbd (23–28 Mar), sourced exclusively from South Korea as Japan withdrew entirely after mid-March. With China's Mar 11 refined fuel export ban and Sinopec cutting runs, internal diesel supply-demand is tightening from both directions.
china_gasoline: Gasoline imports fell to zero by 16 Mar from a marginal ~25 kbd, reflecting China's self-sufficiency in gasoline refining and the broader regional product squeeze as Asian refiners conserve output for domestic markets.
china_naphtha: China's naphtha supply chain underwent a complete structural overhaul: Gulf share collapsed from 77% (2–8 Mar) to zero by 23–28 Mar, with Qatar (172 kbd), Saudi Arabia (73 kbd), and UAE (45 kbd) all disappearing as Hormuz closure and QatarEnergy FM severed traditional feedstock routes.

Russia filled the void at 124 kbd (38% of supply in 23–28 Mar), joined by Norway (89 kbd), South Korea (58 kbd), and Thailand (55 kbd) — a supplier mix unthinkable pre-crisis. Wanhua Chemical's FM on Middle East supplies underscores the downstream impact on China's 2.2 Mtpa ethylene capacity at Yantai.
Total naphtha volumes remain 19% below pre-crisis at 326 kbd — if the Gulf blockade persists into April, petrochemical feedstock rationing becomes inevitable.
india_crude: India's crude imports staged a V-shaped recovery from the 9–15 Mar trough of 3.6 mb/d to 5.1 mb/d (23–28 Mar), exceeding the early-March baseline — Operation Urja Suraksha's deployment of 5+ warships to escort Indian tankers through Hormuz appears to be restoring flow, with 22 priority vessels identified for secure transit.

Russia consolidated as India's dominant supplier, surging from 1.2 mb/d to a peak of 2.6 mb/d (16–22 Mar) before easing to 1.9 mb/d — more than doubling pre-crisis share. Gulf share crashed to just 5.8% in the worst week before rebounding to 34.5% as Saudi Arabia recovered to 1.1 mb/d via Yanbu pipeline bypass.

Iraq effectively exited (653 → 71 kbd) following the Mar 20 Basra FM, while new suppliers Nigeria (481 kbd), Ecuador (352 kbd), and Colombia (317 kbd) appeared in the latest week. India's trade deficit is expected to widen by >$4B per Business Today, but the diversification strategy is delivering early results.

MRPL's FM on gasoline exports and 500 kbd refinery throughput cuts reflect the severity of the early-March shock — but the recovery trajectory suggests India's naval-escort strategy and aggressive non-Gulf procurement are working faster than most analysts expected.
india_lng: India's LNG imports collapsed 46% to 31 ktons/d in mid-March as Qatar's FM — which had supplied 41% of India's LNG — rippled through Petronet LNG's 7.5 Mtpa Dahej terminal. Gujarat Gas's 50% industrial supply cut and Petronet's -9.3% stock crash reflect downstream panic.

Oman's non-Hormuz LNG position proved critical, rebounding to 37.5 ktons/d (23–28 Mar) to anchor India's recovery to 81 ktons/d — actually the strongest week of the month, surpassing the pre-crisis 58 ktons/d baseline as US (22.5 ktons/d) and Nigerian/Angolan cargoes arrived.

The 16–22 Mar week marked the low point with Gulf share at zero and only Nigerian/Angolan spot cargoes arriving. The V-shaped LNG recovery mirrors crude and suggests India's crisis procurement is gaining traction — though spot prices near $25/MMBtu per Moneylife make this diversification expensive.
india_lpg: India's LPG imports halved from 30 ktons/d (2–8 Mar) to a low of 10.5 ktons/d (16–22 Mar) as Gulf NGL supply was severed — the Indian Navy's escort of tankers Pine Gas and Jag Vasant (92,000t combined LPG) through Hormuz under Operation Urja Suraksha enabled partial recovery to 16.2 ktons/d by 23–28 Mar.

The US became the primary non-Gulf LPG supplier at 8.7 ktons/d, but total volumes remain 46% below pre-crisis levels. With ~85% of India's energy imports Gulf-linked, domestic LPG rationing risk remains elevated until Hormuz fully reopens.
india_gasoil_diesel: India's diesel imports pivoted entirely from UAE to Russian supply: UAE sourced the only early-March cargo (8.5 kbd), then Russia became the sole supplier at 34 kbd (16–22 Mar) declining to 18 kbd (23–28 Mar). The shift mirrors the broader Russia-for-Gulf substitution across India's entire energy mix.
india_naphtha: India's naphtha imports underwent near-total supplier overhaul: Gulf share crashed from 100% (2–8 Mar, dominated by UAE 73 kbd and Kuwait 72 kbd) to 52% (23–28 Mar) as Kuwait vanished following its crude FM and Al-Ahmadi refinery drone strikes Mar 19–20.

Russia and Oman divided the replacement supply roughly equally at ~51–54 kbd each, but total volumes at 105 kbd remain 31% below the 152 kbd pre-crisis level — further constraining petrochemical feedstock availability on top of MRPL's 500 kbd refinery run cuts.
japan_crude: Japan's extreme 93.5% Middle East crude dependence is now manifesting as an acute supply crunch: imports held steady at ~2.1 mb/d through 22 Mar as Saudi's Yanbu bypass and UAE's ADCOP pipeline provided alternative loading, but then crashed 55% to just 0.96 mb/d (23–28 Mar) as pipeline-bypass capacity apparently maxed out.

Unlike India and China, Japan has no Russian crude option due to sanctions alignment, leaving it entirely dependent on Gulf producers rerouting through non-Hormuz facilities — Malaysia (58 kbd) was the only non-Gulf crude source in the latest week.

The 80 mb SPR release (54 mb crude + 26 mb products) is now the critical bridge, with ~150 days of reserves remaining per METI. A Daiwa Institute study warns a sustained 10% import cut would push GDP negative — at current rates Japan faces far more than 10%.
japan_lng: Japan's LNG supply proved far more resilient than crude: Australia anchored at 56–95 ktons/d across all four weeks, and a diversified base (Malaysia, Indonesia, PNG, Brunei, Canada) kept volumes at 140 ktons/d (23–28 Mar) — down 25% from early March but avoiding the supply cliff seen in crude.

Qatar was already absent from early March data, and Gulf share fell to zero by 23–28 Mar. Notably, China re-exported 21 ktons/d of LNG to Japan in 16–22 Mar — an unusual intra-Asian cargo reallocation reflecting the severity of Japan's procurement scramble. Nuclear restarts provide incremental demand-side relief.
japan_lpg: Japan achieved complete LPG decoupling from the Gulf: all four weeks sourced 100% from the United States, with volumes actually rising to 34.7 ktons/d (23–28 Mar) from 22 ktons/d early March — pre-crisis diversification now looks prescient as Gulf NGL producers (Saudi, Qatar, Kuwait) are all offline.

The 100% US dependency creates its own concentration risk if Gulf Coast export infrastructure faces any disruption, but for now US terminals at Sabine Pass and Marcus Hook are running at maximum utilization to serve surging Asian demand per Argus.
japan_kero_jet: Japan's jet fuel imports stabilized at ~82 kbd (23–28 Mar) sourced entirely from South Korea, after an early-March dip when China supplied 45 kbd before Beijing's Mar 11 refined fuel export ban severed that route. South Korea's refinery output is providing adequate regional jet fuel for now, but depends on Seoul maintaining product exports.
japan_gasoil_diesel: Japan's diesel imports remained sporadic and low-volume throughout March, peaking briefly at 53 kbd (16–22 Mar) via an unusual Brunei cargo. Domestic diesel supply is primarily met by local refining, but Japan's refinery throughput faces growing crude feedstock constraints given the 55% import crash in late March.
japan_gasoline: Japan's gasoline imports from South Korea surged 7x from 14 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 100 kbd (23–28 Mar), signaling growing domestic refinery shortfalls as crude inputs decline. Seoul's willingness to export gasoline while banning naphtha exports suggests deliberate intra-alliance prioritization of fuel supply over petrochemical feedstock.
japan_naphtha: Japan's naphtha imports swung wildly — 216 kbd to 289 kbd to 86 kbd to 220 kbd across the four weeks — with Gulf sources (primarily UAE and Qatar) still providing 71% in 23–28 Mar, reflecting Japan's deep structural dependence on Gulf NGL for petrochemical feedstock.

Diversification is emerging out of necessity: Peru (22 kbd), Algeria (15 kbd), and South Korea (28 kbd) appeared as non-traditional sources, but Japan's naphtha-dependent petrochemical complexes remain tightly coupled to UAE's ADCOP-fed refineries and face curtailment risk if Hormuz bypass capacity tightens further.
south_korea_crude: South Korea's crude imports fell 56% from 3.5 mb/d to 1.5 mb/d over the month — the steepest decline among the four Asian majors — as Hormuz closure and Saudi/Kuwait pipeline bottlenecks choked supply that was 59% Gulf-sourced pre-crisis.

The US emerged as a critical swing supplier, peaking at 807 kbd (16–22 Mar), while Algeria appeared as an unusual new source (180 kbd in 23–28 Mar). Seoul's 'Crisis Level 3' protocol, government freight subsidies for Cape of Good Hope routing, and 22.5 mb SPR release (6.0 mb drawn by Mar 28) are enabling some diversification but cannot bridge the ~2 mb/d shortfall.

The Mar 27 naphtha export ban signals Seoul expects a prolonged disruption and is prioritizing domestic feedstock. YNCC's FM on ethylene production due to 'total Middle East naphtha suspension' underscores the cascading petrochemical impact — with 208 days of crude reserves, Korea's buffer is deep but crude is only part of the vulnerability.
south_korea_lng: South Korea's pre-crisis LNG diversification is paying dividends: Qatar never exceeded 11% of supply in any week, and the latest week (23–28 Mar) shows zero Gulf LNG yet total imports only declined 22% to 106 ktons/d — well within manageable territory thanks to Australia (56 ktons/d), Indonesia, Malaysia, and the new KOGAS-Trafigura 3.3 mtpa deal for US LNG.

With 80% of LNG under long-term contracts per S&P Global, Seoul has credibly stated it sees 'no LNG shortages' — a position that contrasts sharply with its crude vulnerability and validates the decade-long strategy of contract diversification.
south_korea_lpg: Like Japan, South Korea achieved 100% North American LPG sourcing (US + Canada) throughout the crisis with zero Gulf exposure. However, volumes declined from 15 ktons/d to 8 ktons/d (23–28 Mar), suggesting logistics constraints as US Gulf Coast terminals struggle to meet surging pan-Asian demand simultaneously.
The LPG-naphtha price spread has shifted as both feedstocks face Gulf-linked supply constraints per ICIS, potentially forcing PDH plant operators to reassess feedstock economics.
south_korea_gasoil_diesel: South Korea's diesel imports remained minimal throughout March, sourced from regional partners (Japan, China) and notably including Russian supply (8.8 kbd) in 23–28 Mar — a small but visible indicator of the sanctions-vs-energy-security tension as Asia's fifth-largest crude importer operates in crisis procurement mode.
south_korea_gasoline: Gasoline imports fell from ~108 kbd (9–15 Mar, from Japan) to zero by 23–28 Mar, consistent with domestic refineries maintaining gasoline output even as crude inputs decline — aided by the Mar 27 naphtha export ban that freed refinery capacity for fuel production over petrochemical feedstock.
south_korea_naphtha: South Korea's naphtha imports — critical for its world-scale petrochemical industry — saw Gulf share plummet from 56% to 30% (23–28 Mar), with UAE and Bahrain disappearing while Oman (81 kbd) held as the last major Gulf supplier via its non-Hormuz position.

A remarkable geographic diversification is underway: Greece (73 kbd), Algeria (62 kbd), Peru (58 kbd), Egypt (55 kbd), and Libya (31 kbd) all appeared as naphtha suppliers in the latest week — Mediterranean and Latin American sources replacing Gulf NGL entirely.

YNCC's FM on ethylene citing 'total Middle East naphtha suspension' was declared prematurely — naphtha is arriving, just from completely different origins at higher cost. The question is whether these alternative flows can sustain the ~600+ kbd South Korea needs for its petrochemical complex long-term.
thailand_crude: Thailand's crude imports fell to ~0.88 mb/d (23–28 Mar) from ~0.98 mb/d pre-crisis, but the supplier mix inverted: UAE now supplies 93% of all inflows — up from 33% in early March — as ADCOP pipeline deliveries via Fujairah bypass the Hormuz closure entirely.

Bangkok secured a bilateral Hormuz transit deal with Iran on Mar 28, joining India, Pakistan, and Malaysia as 'friendly nations' — but the data shows Thailand had already pivoted almost exclusively to UAE crude before the deal was signed, suggesting the agreement is insurance rather than an active trade enabler.

Pre-crisis diversity (US, Indonesia, Brazil, Libya, Australia, Kuwait) has collapsed to just two suppliers (UAE + Malaysia) by late March — a concentration risk that will persist until Hormuz reopens or long-haul Atlantic Basin cargoes begin arriving.
thailand_lng: Qatar's LNG force majeure eliminated 52% of Thailand's LNG supply overnight — deliveries dropped from 27.4 ktons/d in early March to zero from 9 Mar onward, consistent with QatarEnergy's blanket FM on all 77 Mtpa of capacity.

Australia has become Thailand's LNG anchor, rising to 12.6 ktons/d (23–28 Mar) alongside new suppliers Mozambique, Canada, and Oman (outside Hormuz) — but total volumes at 35.5 ktons/d remain 32% below the pre-crisis 52.4 ktons/d, per Kpler tracking, compressing power generation margins as spot prices near $24/MMBtu.
thailand_gasoil_diesel: Thailand's diesel imports are low-volume and entirely non-Gulf sourced — a one-off 45.5 kbd/d Indian cargo (16–22 Mar) aside, flows average under 10 kbd/d from regional suppliers (Malaysia, China), indicating domestic refinery output is largely covering demand.
thailand_naphtha: Thailand's naphtha imports collapsed 97% from 114 kbd/d (2–8 Mar) to just 3.7 kbd/d (23–28 Mar) after Bahrain's BAPCO Sitra refinery — which supplied 63–100% of inflows — was destroyed by missile strikes and declared FM on all operations.

No replacement supplier has materialized at scale: the only cargo in late March was 3.7 kbd/d from Singapore, leaving Thai petrochemical crackers critically short of feedstock with no near-term recovery path until Atlantic Basin naphtha cargoes can be sourced.
vietnam_crude: Vietnam's crude imports collapsed to zero (23–28 Mar) from 286 kbd/d (~0.29 mb/d) pre-crisis — the most severe supply wipeout among Asian importers — driven by its near-total dependence on Kuwait, which declared FM on crude exports after the Al-Ahmadi refinery drone strikes.

Hanoi's emergency response — cutting MFN oil import tariffs to 0% via Decree 72 on Mar 9, and securing 4 million barrels from international partners — produced only a brief 88 kbd/d Nigerian cargo (16–22 Mar) before imports flatlined, per Kpler. The government's Mar 23 energy cooperation deal with Russia signals desperation for longer-term supply, but physical cargoes are weeks away.

With Nghi Son Refinery (~200 kbd capacity) dependent on Kuwaiti feedstock, Vietnam faces a cascading refinery shutdown risk that will force increased reliance on imported refined products — already visible in the 75% surge in South Korean diesel imports.
vietnam_kero_jet: Vietnam's jet fuel imports halved from 33 kbd/d to 16 kbd/d through March, sourced entirely from regional suppliers (China, Thailand, Singapore) with zero Gulf exposure — but declining volumes suggest tightening availability as regional refineries cut runs amid their own crude supply constraints.
vietnam_gasoil_diesel: As crude imports collapsed, Vietnam pivoted hard to refined product imports: gasoil/diesel surged 75% from 96 kbd/d (2–8 Mar) to 168 kbd/d (23–28 Mar), with South Korea supplying 91% of volumes — a direct substitute for lost Nghi Son Refinery output.

This crude-to-products import substitution is a costly workaround: Vietnam is paying refined product premiums rather than crude processing margins, compressing downstream economics while Korean refiners capture the spread.
vietnam_gasoline: Vietnam's gasoline imports fell 78% from 63 kbd/d to 14 kbd/d through March, with Singapore as the sole remaining supplier by late March — the sharp decline reflects both tightening regional supply and Vietnam's worsening ability to pay for increasingly expensive spot cargoes amid the energy crisis.
united_states_crude: US crude imports averaged ~2.0 mb/d (23–28 Mar), down from ~2.75 mb/d in early March, with Gulf-sourced barrels (Saudi + Iraq) comprising 31% — Saudi Arabia remains the top single supplier at 571 kbd/d, likely routing via Yanbu/Red Sea rather than Hormuz.

Iraq's share collapsed from 368 kbd/d (2–8 Mar) to just 47 kbd/d (23–28 Mar), consistent with the Basra FM that shut foreign-operated oilfields and cut output to ~800 kbd/d from 3.3 mb/d — the residual trickle likely represents pre-crisis cargoes in transit.

Western Hemisphere suppliers now dominate: Venezuela (387 kbd/d), Mexico (259 kbd/d), and a rotating cast of Colombia, Ecuador, Argentina, Guyana, and Brazil collectively provide ~70% of US crude imports — the SPR release of 45.2 mb (of 172 mb committed) at ~1.4 mb/d is supplementing but not replacing seaborne flows.
united_states_kero_jet: US jet fuel imports spiked 15x from 13 kbd/d (2–8 Mar) to 86 kbd/d (23–28 Mar) as South Korea emerged as the primary supplier at 67 kbd/d — reflecting both rising Asian refined product exports as Gulf crude dries up and potential US refinery margin optimization shifting yields away from jet.
Taiwan appeared as a new jet fuel supplier (19 kbd/d in 23–28 Mar), an unusual flow suggesting CPC Corp is exporting surplus refined products as its own crude supply shifts to US barrels.
united_states_gasoil_diesel: US diesel imports halved from 179 kbd/d to 89 kbd/d through March, with Canada providing 90% of flows — Turkey dropped out after mid-March as Hormuz disruption likely redirected Turkish refinery output to higher-priced Mediterranean markets. The US remains broadly self-sufficient on middle distillates with domestic refinery output offsetting the import decline.
united_states_gasoline: US gasoline imports fell 38% to 209 kbd/d (23–28 Mar) from 335 kbd/d pre-crisis, sourced entirely from non-Gulf origins (Canada, Bahamas, UK, USVI) — the transatlantic European flow (Netherlands, Belgium in early March) disappeared by late March.

India briefly appeared as a gasoline supplier (49 kbd/d in 16–22 Mar), an unusual flow likely representing pre-FM cargoes before MRPL declared force majeure on gasoline exports and cut 500 kbd of refinery runs.
united_states_naphtha: US naphtha import sources shifted dramatically from European suppliers (France, Netherlands, Belgium) in early March to South American and African origins (Angola 58 kbd/d, Argentina 18 kbd/d, Brazil 17 kbd/d) by 23–28 Mar, as European naphtha tightened on the continent's own Gulf supply constraints and refiners hoarded feedstock.
taiwan_crude: Taiwan completed the most dramatic supply pivot in this crisis: Gulf share crashed from 99.3% (2–8 Mar) to 0% (23–28 Mar), with the US becoming the sole crude supplier at 343 kbd/d (~0.34 mb/d) — a historic shift for an economy that sourced over 90% of crude from the Middle East pre-crisis.

Total volumes fell 18% to 0.34 mb/d from 0.42 mb/d pre-crisis, but the 120-day strategic reserve provides buffer. The critical risk is sustainability: US crude exports to Taiwan must transit the Pacific (~25-day voyage vs. ~15 days from the Gulf), straining tanker availability as every Asian importer competes for non-Gulf barrels.

Evergreen Marine's FM on all Gulf bookings and Kuwait's disappearance after W11 confirm that Hormuz closure has severed Taiwan's traditional supply chain entirely — CPC Corp's pivot to US WTI-linked barrels will compress refining margins given the Brent-WTI spread.
taiwan_lng: Taiwan's LNG diversification is the batch's success story: total imports actually rose to 83.6 ktons/d (23–28 Mar) from 50.1 ktons/d pre-crisis, even as Qatar dropped from 8.9 ktons/d to zero after the FM declaration — Taipei aggressively secured spot cargoes from Australia (48.3 ktons/d, up 5x), Equatorial Guinea, Brunei, and Oman.

Qatar's exit after W11 was offset by Australia nearly quintupling its supply share, consistent with reports that Taiwan is among Asian buyers scrambling to lock in 2+ months of LNG. Oman (outside Hormuz) provides a steady 11 ktons/d baseload, but at spot prices near $24/MMBtu, Taiwan's power generation costs have roughly doubled.

With Taiwan's 120-day oil reserve noted as the lowest among major Asian importers, the ability to maintain LNG flows is existential for a grid that derives ~40% of electricity from gas — any disruption to the Australian or US LNG pipeline would trigger immediate power rationing.
taiwan_gasoil_diesel: Taiwan's diesel imports are small and sporadic (0–97 kbd/d through March), sourced from South Korea, China, and a brief Russian cargo — reflecting adequate domestic refinery coverage. The appearance of Russian diesel (3.8 kbd/d in 16–22 Mar) signals Taiwan's pragmatic approach to supply security despite geopolitical sensitivities.
taiwan_gasoline: Taiwan's gasoline imports are minimal and intermittent — a single 25 kbd/d Japanese cargo (16–22 Mar) was the only inflow all month, consistent with CPC Corp's domestic refineries covering demand. No Gulf exposure.
taiwan_naphtha: Taiwan's naphtha imports collapsed to zero (23–28 Mar) from 176 kbd/d in early March, following a brief mid-March pivot to Gulf sources (UAE 128 kbd/d, Iraq 20 kbd/d in W11) that proved unsustainable as Hormuz tightened further and Iraq declared FM.

The naphtha wipeout threatens Taiwan's $30B+ petrochemical sector: Formosa Plastics and other crackers face feedstock starvation with no replacement supplier established, and Singapore/US cargoes from early March did not continue — urgent long-haul procurement from the Atlantic Basin is now critical.
